items.title: Tehran signals mass crackdown despite mass protests and threats from Trump | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Fresh protests have broken out in Iran despite an intensifying crackdown and internet blackout. Human rights monitors say at least 72 people have been killed and 2,300 arrested. The protests are posing one of the biggest challenges to the clerical government in its history. US President Trump is offering support to the demonstrators.

items.title: Tehran mosque set on fire: Could it fuel state military repression against protesters? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Observers say fear is no longer working in Iran. As authorities intensify their crackdown on protesters, the street demonstrations have been growing.  A hospital in Tehran has reported over 217 casualties.
Iranians say the economic and political conditions are too harsh to take lying down. US Secretary of State Marco Rubio says the United States supports the brave people of Iran, who want change. Authorities are keeping the country in the dark, with the internet down for another day.


Chapter Breakdown

items.title: Tectonic forces in Ethiopia are carving a new ocean across the continent
items.author: Jamal Osman
items.content:
The movement of tectonic plates may take millions of years, but in Northern Ethiopia, you can see it happen in real time.

In recent years, the Afar region has been hit with earthquakes and volcanic eruptions, and huge cracks have suddenly appeared which run for miles.

It’s the start of the formation of a new ocean, which will one day split Africa in two.

items.title: Dozens killed as Iran protests persist despite internet shutdown
items.author: Harry Fawcett
items.content:
Dozens of people are reported to have been killed and more than 2,000 arrested during the last two weeks of protests across Iran, according to a human rights group.

The authorities have continued to impose an internet blackout, cutting off access to mobile phones as well.

But that doesn’t appear to have stopped the protestors.

items.title: Son of last Iranian shah urges protesters to shut down cities | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Unrest continues to grow in Iran, with street demonstrations raging across the country. Protesters are publicly calling for the end of the Islamic Republic. Reza Pahlavi, the son of the last shah of Iran, has sent words of encouragement to the protesters - saying Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei is trembling in fear. But so far authorities have not backed down. The internet is still cut off, with rights groups alleging more and more deaths and reports of overwhelmed hospitals. #dwnewsshorts

items.title: Why New Zealand Abolished Farm Subsidies
items.author: Bloomberg Television
items.content:

items.title: How vulnerable is Iran's regime as protests show no sign of slowing? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Unrest continues to grow in Iran, with street demonstrations raging in cities across the country. Protesters have been publicly calling for the end of the Islamic Republic - as its rulers look more vulnerable than during previous popular uprisings. The exiled crown prince Reza Pahlavi has now called on demonstrators to seize Iranian city centers. Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei has refused to back down. Authorities blacked out the internet for a second night in a row as rights groups say dozens of people have been killed.

00:00 Protests continue in Iran
00:37 DW speaks with political analyst Cornelius Adebahr, associate fellow with the German Council on Foreign Relations

items.title: Morocco, Senegal roar to AFCON semis
items.author: euronews
items.content:
<article>Morocco, Senegal roar to AFCON semis

Iliman Ndiaye’s first-half strike was enough for Senegal to beat 10-man Mali 1-0 in the first quarterfinal. Brahim Díaz scored again and host Morocco joined Senegal in the semifinals of the Africa Cup of Nations with a 2-0 win over Cameroon on Friday.

items.title: With millions of Americans on GLP-1s...what are they eating?
items.author: Bloomberg Television
items.content:
<article>Ozempic and similar weight loss drugs, known as GLP-1s, make people less hungry, but they don't seem to stop them from going out to eat.
Bloomberg Businessweek’s Deena Shanker joins Stacey Vanek Smith and Max Chafkin on the Everybody's Business podcast to unpack this new phenomenon and why the weight loss drug craze is actually benefiting the restaurant industry http://bit.ly/46drPxC</article>

items.title: Why car brands keep calling tech giants for help | The Dip
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>CES has become a highlight for the car industry as software becomes critical. Nvidia is rolling out autonomous-driving software that Mercedes plans to ship in new models, highlighting how carmakers are leaning on tech giants to move faster.  Sony and Honda’s Afeela is doubling down on a “car as entertainment” idea, with big screens and gaming features aimed at premium buyers.

items.title: Mercosur: How Europe aims to reposition its trade in the Trump era | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>A broad majority of European Union countries have approved the signing of a deal with the South American trading bloc, Mercosur. It would be the EU's biggest free-trade agreement to date. The pact with the four founding Mercosur members -- Brazil, Argentina, Uruguay and Paraguay -- was supposed to have been signed in December. Italy originally blocked it, but finally backed the deal. Opposition led by France and Poland, whose farmers marched again on Friday, was not enough to sink the pact. Ireland, Hungary and Austria also opposed it. The signing ceremony is planned for January 17th.

00:00 EU Parliament approves signing of Mercosur trade deal
00:40 DW speaks with Rebecca Christie, senior Fellow at Bruegel - a Brussels-based economic policy think tank - and host of Bruegel's podcast, The Sound of Economics
04:00 DW speaks with Thiago de Aragao,  director of strategy at consulting company Arko Advice in Washington

items.title: Why Germany won't openly condemn Trump's Venezuela strike | Berlin Briefing Podcast
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Headlines coming out of the US shook the world in the first week of 2026: the military operation in Venezuela and Trump's talk of seizing Greenland confronted European leaders with an uncomfortable question: How do democracies navigate a world where power politics trumps international law? DW Berlin Briefing host Nina Haase and her guests, Michaela Küfner (DW Chief Political Editor) and Thorsten Benner (Global Public Policy Institute) discuss whether Germany's relative silence is strategic restraint or helplessness in the face of US assertiveness.
